edges.description: Airway hyperresponsiveness provides the substrate for bronchoconstriction in response to numerous diverse stimuli.
Type 2 cytokines drive eosinophilic airway inflammation and mast cell activation.
IL-13 directly induces goblet cell metaplasia and mucin gene expression.
Bronchoconstriction and airflow limitation produce wheeze as a frequent asthma symptom.
Airflow limitation from bronchoconstriction contributes to chest tightness.
Severe acute asthma can produce hyperventilation with increased breathing rate and depth.
Severe bronchoconstriction and acute asthma exacerbation can progress to respiratory distress and respiratory failure.
Severe asthma with progressive hypoxia can produce cyanosis as a hypoxemia-related manifestation.
Exertion can trigger or magnify asthma symptoms, limiting physical activity.
Exertion-triggered asthma symptoms reduce exercise tolerance and physical activity.
Asthma symptom burden and dyspnea correlate with fatigue.
Sudden asthma attacks and anticipation of future attacks generate anxiety.
Excess mucus production contributes to recurrent cough in asthma.
Nocturnal airway narrowing and mucus-related cough can disrupt sleep.
SIRT1-mediated deacetylation inhibits NF-κB and STAT3 signaling, reducing Th2 cell differentiation and type 2 cytokine production.

nodes.description: Chronic inflammation leads to airway hyperresponsiveness and recurrent episodes of wheezing, breathlessness, chest tightness, and coughing.

evidence.reference_title: Mechanisms in allergic airway inflammation - lessons from studies in the mouse.
evidence.snippet: The pathogenesis of allergic airway inflammation is complex, involving multiple cell types such as T helper 2 cells, regulatory T cells, eosinophils, dendritic cells, mast cells, and parenchymal cells of the lung.

evidence.reference_title: Late-phase reaction in asthma: basic mechanisms.
evidence.snippet: The numbers of eosinophils and neutrophils are increased 4 h after challenge with allergen in the broncho-alveolar lavage fluid (BAL) of asthmatics who show an LPR.

evidence.reference_title: Basophils and mast cells in airway inflammation and asthma.
evidence.snippet: Accumulation of basophils and mast cells in airway tissues during allergic and allergic-type inflammatory responses, including asthma, is one of the hallmarks of these disease processes.

evidence.reference_title: Airway hyperresponsiveness in asthma: The role of the epithelium.
evidence.snippet: Airway hyperresponsiveness (AHR) is a key clinical feature of asthma. The presence of AHR in people with asthma provides the substrate for bronchoconstriction in response to numerous diverse stimuli, contributing to airflow limitation and symptoms including breathlessness, wheeze, and chest tightness.

evidence.reference_title: Cardiac asthma: new insights into an old disease.
evidence.snippet: Pulmonary edema and pulmonary vascular congestion have been thought to be the primary causes of cardiac asthma but most patients have a poor response to diuretics... Evidence suggests that circulating inflammatory factors and tissue growth factors also lead to airway obstruction suggesting the possibility of developing novel therapies.

evidence.reference_title: Asthma, inflammation, eosinophils and bronchial hyperresponsiveness.
evidence.snippet: Asthmatics can have a blood eosinophilia which in some studies correlates with the severity of the disease... The eosinophils that contribute to asthma will be those in the lung.

evidence.reference_title: Non-asthmatic eosinophilic bronchitis and its relationship with asthma.
evidence.snippet: NAEB shares similar eosinophilic inflammation of airway and response to corticosteroids with asthma.

evidence.reference_title: Exercise-induced asthma.
evidence.snippet: Exercise-induced asthma can appear as one of many forms of airway hyperreactivity or as a unique clinical entity.

nodes.description: Long-standing inflammation can lead to structural changes in the airway, permanently affecting lung function.

evidence.reference_title: Pathological airway remodelling in inflammation.
evidence.snippet: In asthma, it includes alterations of the epithelial cell layer with goblet cell hyperplasia, thickening of basement membranes, peri-bronchial and peri-bronchoalveolar fibrosis.

evidence.reference_title: Consequences of long-term inflammation. The natural history of asthma.
evidence.snippet: In the long-term, asthma may become moderately to fully irreversible. Severe, irreversible airflow obstruction may develop despite apparently appropriate therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Structural changes in the airways in asthma: observations and consequences.
evidence.snippet: Structural changes reported in the airways of asthmatics include epithelial fragility, goblet cell hyperplasia, enlarged submucosal mucus glands, angiogenesis, increased matrix deposition in the airway wall, increased airway smooth muscle mass, wall thickening and abnormalities in elastin.

evidence.reference_title: Airway inflammation and remodeling in asthma: current concepts.
evidence.snippet: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disorder of the airways interacting with altered structure and function of the formed elements including smooth muscle.

evidence.reference_title: The cell biology of asthma.
evidence.snippet: Two airway cell types are critical for asthma pathogenesis: epithelial cells and smooth muscle cells.

evidence.reference_title: Reviewing asthma and anxiety.
evidence.snippet: Both the attacks themselves and the prospect of attacks generate much anxiety amongst patients.

evidence.reference_title: The association between asthma and anxiety in elementary school students in Japan.
evidence.snippet: Asthma was significantly associated with anxiety in boys, particularly in older boys.

evidence.reference_title: Astma jako choroba psychosomatyczna.
evidence.snippet: Another aspect is the relationship of bronchial asthma with the development of mood disorders.

nodes.description: During an asthma attack, smooth muscles around the airways tighten and narrow the airway.

evidence.reference_title: Mechanical determinants of airways hyperresponsiveness.
evidence.snippet: Breathing difficulties are caused by excessive narrowing of the pulmonary airways, which is instigated by shortening of the airway smooth muscle (ASM).

evidence.reference_title: Statins in Asthma: A Closer Look into the Pharmacological Mechanism of Action.
evidence.snippet: In acute and chronic allergen driven animal models of asthma, statins reduce airway hyper-responsiveness, inflammation and remodeling.

evidence.reference_title: Airway smooth muscle as a regulator of immune responses and bronchomotor tone.
evidence.snippet: ASM plays an important role in regulating bronchomotor tone, in perpetuating airway inflammation, and in remodeling of the airways.

evidence.reference_title: Treating asthma means treating airway smooth muscle cells.
evidence.snippet: Airway smooth muscle cells are known to be the main effector cells of airway narrowing.

evidence.snippet: CONCLUSIONS: Asthma severity was dependent on variety of symptoms, consisting mostly of wheezing, breathlessness, chest tightness, and cough.

evidence.snippet: Asthma is a chronic disease of the airways characterized by inflammation, tightened muscles, and thickened airway walls leading to symptoms such as shortness of breath, chest tightness, and cough in patients.

evidence.reference_title: Chronic cough.
evidence.snippet: Chronic cough is associated with poor quality of life and can negatively affect quality of life. Diseases causing chronic cough are most commonly asthma, eosinophilic bronchitis, gastroesophageal reflux, post-nasal drip syndrome, rhinosinusitis, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, pulmonary fibrosis, and bronchiectasis.

evidence.reference_title: [Differentiation of dyspnea in patients with asthma and lung sarcoidosis].
evidence.snippet: Dyspnea as a subjective feeling of lack of air is the patients with the pathology of the respiratory system. Objectivization of this phenomenon is difficult, despite the introduction of questionnaires about the degree of dyspnoea. The results of these assessments do not always correlate with the results of spirometric tests. In patients with asthma, dyspnoea is caused by airway obstruction.

nodes.description: Viral lower respiratory wheezing illnesses in infancy and early childhood, particularly those caused by human rhinovirus (and to a lesser extent respiratory syncytial virus), are among the strongest early-life predictors of subsequent childhood asthma. In a high-risk prospective birth cohort, wheezing with rhinovirus in the first three years of life conferred an almost ten-fold increase in the odds of asthma at age 6, an effect that grew stronger when wheezing occurred later in early childhood. Rhinovirus infection of the developing airway epithelium interacts with allergic (type 2) sensitization to injure and remodel the epithelium and bias the immune response toward the Th2 program, contributing to asthma inception rather than acting purely as a marker of pre-existing susceptibility. This positions early respiratory infection as an upstream, potentially modifiable contributor to the chronic airway inflammation that defines established asthma.
nodes.id: Early-Life Rhinovirus Wheezing Illness and Asthma Inception

evidence.snippet: Fatigue as a symptom is common in asthmatic patients and correlates with asthma-related quality of life, asthma control, dyspnea, depression and anxiety.

evidence.snippet: Future studies are needed to better understand the physical, psychological, behavioural and systemic factors that precipitate or perpetuate fatigue in asthma.

nodes.description: Group 1 house dust mite (HDM) allergens, exemplified by the cysteine peptidase Der p 1, are potent triggers of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation in airway epithelial cells, an event relevant to asthma because the disease is associated with deficits in antioxidant defences and because ROS amplify inflammation through transcription-factor and chromatin regulation. Der p 1 behaves as a prothrombinase, generating thrombin that activates protease-activated receptors (PAR1/PAR4); the resulting signaling, converging with purinoceptor (P2X7/P2Y) activation by ATP released through pannexons, drives sheddase activity of the metalloprotease ADAM10. ADAM10 in turn sustains further prothrombin activation, establishing a self-amplifying thrombin-ROS signaling cycle. As the principal sheddase of the adherens-junction protein E-cadherin, ADAM10 activation also compounds the epithelial-barrier disruption caused by direct cleavage of tight junctions by group 1 HDM allergens, and ADAM10 liberates chemokines (CCL20, CCL2, CCL5, CXCL8) and promotes TH2 bias and IgE synthesis (via CD23 shedding) - tying allergen-protease sensing to the type 2 inflammatory program of allergic asthma.
nodes.id: House Dust Mite Allergen Protease-Induced Epithelial Oxidant Signaling

evidence.reference_title: Allergen-dependent oxidant formation requires purinoceptor activation of ADAM 10 and prothrombin.
evidence.snippet: Der p 1, a cysteine peptidase, behaves as a prothrombinase, thereby triggering canonical activation of protease-activated receptor (PAR) 1 and 4 by thrombin.

evidence.reference_title: Allergen-dependent oxidant formation requires purinoceptor activation of ADAM 10 and prothrombin.
evidence.snippet: Our data implicate purinoceptor-dependent activation of ADAM 10 as a downstream effector of ROS production in an innate response to HDM allergens.

evidence.reference_title: Allergen-dependent oxidant formation requires purinoceptor activation of ADAM 10 and prothrombin.
evidence.snippet: ADAM 10 establishes a signaling cycle capable of sustaining prothrombin activation after its initiation by group 1 HDM allergens.

nodes.description: SIRT1 (Sirtuin 1), an NAD+-dependent deacetylase, exerts protective effects in asthma by inhibiting pro-inflammatory signaling pathways (NF-κB, STAT3) while activating stress-response transcription factors (Nrf2, FOXO3) and suppressing pro-fibrotic TGF-β/Smad signaling. SIRT1 activation attenuates Th2 inflammation, suppresses mucus hypersecretion and goblet cell metaplasia, and reduces airway smooth muscle proliferation. SIRT1 also mitigates oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction.
nodes.id: SIRT1-Mediated NAD+ Signaling and Protective Deacetylation

evidence.snippet: Results from analyses using structural equation modeling revealed an association between worsening asthma-related lung function and poor sleep quality in the full sample, as well as better asthma-related lung function at night and more optimal sleep efficiency that night.

evidence.reference_title: Understanding the Relationship Between Asthma and Sleep in the Pediatric Population.
evidence.snippet: The prevalence of nocturnal symptoms of asthma is high, and most children regularly experience nighttime symptoms.

nodes.description: Allergic asthma is driven by a type 2 immune response in which allergen-activated dendritic cells promote Th2 cell differentiation. Th2 cells and ILC2s produce IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13, which drive IgE class switching, eosinophil recruitment, goblet cell metaplasia, and airway hyperresponsiveness. IL-4/IL-13 signal through STAT6 to amplify the type 2 program.

evidence.reference_title: Allergic Endotypes and Phenotypes of Asthma.
evidence.snippet: It is a TH2-driven process. Biomarkers have been identified to distinguish patients with allergic asthma, particularly serum IgE levels, tests to indicate sensitization to aeroallergens such as specific IgE or skin prick test positivity, blood and sputum eosinophil levels

evidence.reference_title: Mechanisms in allergic airway inflammation - lessons from studies in the mouse.
evidence.snippet: The pathogenesis of allergic airway inflammation is complex, involving multiple cell types such as T helper 2 cells, regulatory T cells, eosinophils, dendritic cells, mast cells, and parenchymal cells of the lung. The cellular response in allergic airway inflammation is controlled by a broad range of bioactive mediators, including IgE, cytokines and chemokines.

evidence.reference_title: Emerging role of SIRT1 in asthma and COPD from molecular mechanisms to translational therapy.
evidence.snippet: In asthma, SIRT1 attenuates Th2 inflammation, mucus hypersecretion, and airway smooth muscle proliferation

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypes of childhood asthma: are they real?
evidence.snippet: It has been suggested that there are several distinct phenotypes of childhood asthma or childhood wheezing. Here, we review the research relating to these phenotypes, with a focus on the methods used to define and validate them.

evidence.reference_title: Wheezes.
evidence.snippet: Wheezes are usual clinical signs in patients with obstructive airway diseases and particularly during acute episodes of asthma.

evidence.reference_title: Exercise-induced asthma, anaphylaxis, and urticaria.
evidence.snippet: Exercise-induced asthma is a common but frequently undiagnosed problem. The patient may not wheeze, but rather have shortness of breath, chest tightening, and coughing.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnostic challenges of adult asthma.
evidence.snippet: The syndrome of asthma contains a number of different phenotypes that offer the possibility of personalized medicine based on the respective asthma phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Steroid responsiveness and wheezing phenotypes.
evidence.snippet: Oral corticosteroids are the cornerstone of management of acute moderate or severe asthma whilst preventive inhaled corticosteroids are the mainstay of the preventive management of children with asthma. Yet, variation in the magnitude of response to corticosteroids has been observed.

evidence.reference_title: Not all that Wheezes is Asthma.
evidence.snippet: We want to highlight the presentation of a 75-year-old female who was initially managed as asthma and subsequently diagnosed with concurrent excessive dynamic airway collapse (EDAC).
